section.title: The Table of Nations
section.sectionRationale: This chapter is a single, deliberate document: the account of Noah's sons and the seventy peoples that came from them. It maps the whole known world back to one family, setting the stage for Babel and for God's choice of one line, Shem, through which all these nations will be blessed.
section.thesis: From Noah's three sons the whole earth is repopulated, so that every nation on the map is one family under God, and the story is quietly narrowing toward the line of Shem.
section.frameNote: Four movements framed by the heading and closing formula: the introduction; the sons of Japheth; the sons of Ham, including Nimrod; and the sons of Shem, the covenant line.

verses.text: This is the account of Noah’s sons Shem, Ham, and Japheth, who also had sons after the flood.
The sons of Japheth: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras.
The sons of Gomer: Ashkenaz, Riphath, and Togarmah.
And the sons of Javan: Elishah, Tarshish, the Kittites, and the Rodanites.
From these, the maritime peoples separated into their territories, according to their languages, by clans within their nations.
The sons of Ham: Cush, Mizraim, Put, and Canaan.
The sons of Cush: Seba, Havilah, Sabtah, Raamah, and Sabteca. And the sons of Raamah: Sheba and Dedan.
Cush was the father of Nimrod, who began to be a {{gibbor|mighty one}} on the earth.
He was a mighty hunter before the LORD; so it is said, “Like Nimrod, a mighty hunter before the LORD.”
His kingdom began in Babylon, Erech, Accad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar.
From that land he went forth into Assyria, where he built Nineveh, Rehoboth-Ir, Calah,
and Resen, which is between Nineveh and the great city of Calah.
Mizraim was the father of the Ludites, the Anamites, the Lehabites, the Naphtuhites,
the Pathrusites, the Casluhites (from whom the Philistines came), and the Caphtorites.
And Canaan was the father of Sidon his firstborn, and of the Hittites,
the Jebusites, the Amorites, the Girgashites,
the Hivites, the Arkites, the Sinites,
the Arvadites, the Zemarites, and the Hamathites. Later the Canaanite clans were scattered,
and the borders of Canaan extended from Sidon toward Gerar as far as Gaza, and then toward Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, and Zeboiim, as far as Lasha.
These are the sons of Ham according to their clans, languages, lands, and nations.
And sons were also born to Shem, the older brother of Japheth; Shem was the forefather of all the sons of {{eber|Eber}}.
The sons of Shem: Elam, Asshur, Arphaxad, Lud, and Aram.
The sons of Aram: Uz, Hul, Gether, and Mash.
Arphaxad was the father of Shelah, and Shelah was the father of Eber.
Two sons were born to Eber: One was named {{peleg|Peleg}}, because in his days the earth was divided, and his brother was named Joktan.
And Joktan was the father of Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, Jerah,
Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah,
Obal, Abimael, Sheba,
Ophir, Havilah, and Jobab. All these were sons of Joktan.
Their territory extended from Mesha to Sephar, in the eastern hill country.
These are the sons of Shem, according to their clans, languages, lands, and nations.
All these are the clans of Noah’s sons, according to their generations and nations. From these the {{goy|nations}} of the earth spread out after the flood.

gibbor.gloss: A mighty man, warrior, or hero. Nimrod is the first person described this way after the flood, a builder of cities and empires including Babylon. The word can be admiring or ominous, and here it hints at the rise of proud human power that reaches its peak at Babel in the next chapter.

eber.gloss: The ancestor from whose name the word Hebrew is generally derived. By singling out Shem as the forefather of the sons of Eber, the writer flags the family line that leads to Abram the Hebrew and, through him, to Israel and Christ.

peleg.gloss: The name Peleg means division, and it is explained by the note that in his days the earth was divided. This likely points to the scattering at Babel, so the genealogy already carries a trace of the judgment about to be narrated.

goy.gloss: A nation or people, plural goyim. This is the word that will echo through the whole Bible for the nations, or Gentiles. Here every goy on the map is traced to one family, so the later promise that all nations will be blessed through Abram gathers up this very list.

basic.context: After the flood, the world had to be repopulated, and this chapter is like a family map showing where all the nations came from. It traces seventy peoples back to Noah's three sons: Japheth, Ham, and Shem. The big question it answers is simple: where did all the different nations come from?
basic.hermeneutics: The key question is why the Bible gives us a long list of names and places. It is not just a record; it is making a point, that every nation on earth is one family under God. It also slows down over Shem's line, quietly steering us toward the family God will choose.
basic.originalLanguages: Nimrod is called a mighty one, a word that can mean hero or can hint at proud, empire-building power, and his kingdom starts at Babylon. Shem is called the father of the sons of Eber, the name behind the word Hebrew. And one grandson is named Peleg, meaning division, because the earth was split in his days.
basic.typology: This map of all nations sets up a promise: through one man from Shem's line, God will bless every family listed here. The scattered nations of Genesis 10 are gathered again at Pentecost, when people from many nations hear the gospel in their own tongue, and finally in Revelation, where every nation stands before the throne.
basic.christ: The chapter narrows down to Shem, and Shem's line runs to Abraham and then to Jesus. Jesus is the promised descendant through whom the blessing reaches out to every nation on this list, undoing the scattering and gathering a people from all peoples.
basic.god: God is Lord of all the nations, not just one. He is the God who fills the earth with people just as He commanded, and He rules over Babylon and Nineveh and every clan on the map. He is patiently working out a plan to bless them all.
basic.crossReferences: Paul says God made every nation from one man and set their times and places. At Pentecost the scattered languages are reunited in the gospel. And Revelation shows a countless crowd from every nation gathered before the Lamb.
basic.oneStory: This is the world God so loved: seventy nations spread across the earth, all one family, all wandering from Him. The Bible zooms in on Shem's line because that is where the rescuer will come, the one who will bless every nation on this map and gather the scattered peoples home.

context.literary: Marked off by its own heading, the account of Noah's sons, and its closing formula, the chapter is a carefully structured genealogy that fans out from Noah to roughly seventy nations. It deliberately precedes the Babel story, which then explains how these separate languages and lands came to be.
context.historical: The list reflects the peoples known to ancient Israel, from the maritime nations of the north and west to the powers of Mesopotamia and the Canaanite clans they would encounter in the land. Nimrod's cities, Babylon and Nineveh, name the two great empires that would later oppress God's people.
context.canonical: Genesis 10 supplies the backdrop for the whole biblical story of the nations. The seventy nations here are answered by the seventy sent out by Jesus, by the ingathering at Pentecost, and by the final vision of every nation before the throne.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that all the nations of the earth descend from Noah's one family, to locate Israel's neighbours and enemies on the map, and to funnel attention toward Shem's line, the channel of blessing.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is descriptive, an account of how the world was peopled, not a set of commands. Its abiding truths are the unity of humanity, God's rule over all nations, and His purpose to bless them through one chosen line.

debates.question: How should we read the ordering of the chapter, with Japheth first and Shem last?

views.label: A narrowing focus
views.case: Genesis often lists the non-chosen lines first and clears them away, then lingers on the chosen line last; here Shem comes last and is treated most fully, marking him as the line the story will follow.

views.label: A simple geographic sweep
views.case: Others read the order mainly as a movement across the known world, from the distant northern peoples inward, without strong theological weight.

debates.bearing: Reading it as a deliberate narrowing helps us see that even a table of nations is telling the gospel story, quietly setting up God's choice of Abram from Shem's line.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key words are quiet but load-bearing. Nimrod is the first gibbor, mighty one, and his kingdom rises at Babylon, so proud human power is already gathering before the Babel scene. Shem is singled out as father of the sons of Eber, the name behind Hebrew, flagging the covenant family. And Peleg, division, is explained by the note that the earth was divided, so the scattering to come is already stitched into the family tree, while the closing word goy, nations, is the very word the Abrahamic promise will pick up.

typology.type: The seventy nations of the earth
typology.fulfillment: Gathered again in the gospel: seventy sent by Jesus, the nations united at Pentecost, and every nation before the throne in Revelation.

typology.type: The line of Shem, father of the Hebrews
typology.fulfillment: The channel that runs to Abraham and to Christ, through whom the blessing reaches all the nations listed here.

typology.type: Nimrod and his kingdom at Babylon
typology.fulfillment: A first sketch of the proud world-city, the Babylon that Scripture opposes to the city of God until its fall in Revelation.

study.christ: The whole chapter is a map of the nations Jesus came to save, and it narrows to Shem precisely because the Saviour will come through Shem's line. In Christ the promise to bless all the families of the earth reaches this exact list of peoples. The scattering hinted at in Peleg's name and enacted at Babel is reversed in Him: at Pentecost the nations hear one gospel in many tongues, and in Revelation a countless multitude from every nation on this map stands before the Lamb.
study.god: God is shown to be the God of all nations, ruling the whole earth He has refilled. He keeps His word to Noah, be fruitful and fill the earth, and He presides over the rise of empires like Babylon and Nineveh without surrendering His purpose. He is sovereign over the whole human family and is patiently steering history toward the blessing of every people through one chosen line.

crossReferences.note: From one man God made every nation and set their appointed times and boundaries.
All the families of the earth, this very map of nations, will be blessed through Abram.
At Pentecost people from many nations hear the gospel each in their own language, reversing the scattering.
A great multitude from every nation, tribe, people, and tongue stands before the Lamb.

study.oneStory: Genesis 10 is the family portrait of the whole human race, seventy nations from one father, all now spreading out and drifting from God. The Bible lingers on Shem because that is the thread it will follow, down through Abraham to Jesus, the one who blesses every nation on this map and gathers the scattered peoples into one worshipping family at last.

quiz.q: What is the main point of the Table of Nations?

options.text: Every nation on earth descends from Noah's one family under God.
options.note: The chapter traces all peoples back to a single family and God's rule over them.

options.text: It records the names of Israel's kings.
options.note: There are no kings of Israel here; it lists the descent of the nations.

options.text: It lists the nations mainly to fill space before the next story.
options.note: The list is deliberate and theological, not filler.

options.text: It maps the neighbours and powers Israel would later meet.
options.note: True and useful, but the deeper point is the unity of all nations under God.

quiz.q: Why does the chapter give special attention to Shem's line?

options.text: Because the covenant line to Abraham and Christ runs through Shem.
options.note: Shem is father of the sons of Eber, the line that leads to the Saviour.

options.text: Because Shem had the most children.
options.note: The focus is on the covenant line, not on numbers.

options.text: Because Shem's descendants were the most powerful.
options.note: Nimrod from Ham's line builds the great empires; Shem matters for the promise, not power.

options.text: For no particular reason at all.
options.note: The attention to Shem is deliberate and points to Christ.

quiz.q: Who is Nimrod in this chapter?

options.text: A mighty one whose kingdom began at Babylon in Shinar.
options.note: He is the first great empire-builder, linked to Babylon and Nineveh.

options.text: A faithful worshipper of the LORD.
options.note: The text calls him mighty and a city-builder; it does not commend his faith.

options.text: A son of Shem in the covenant line.
options.note: Nimrod descends from Ham through Cush, not from Shem.

options.text: A hunter and ruler whose cities include Nineveh.
options.note: True, though the striking detail is that his kingdom starts at Babylon.

quiz.q: How are the scattered nations of this chapter finally gathered?

options.text: In Christ, at Pentecost and before the throne, from every nation.
options.note: The gospel reunites the peoples that were scattered.

options.text: Through the empire of Babylon uniting them by force.
options.note: Babylon divides and oppresses; it is not the true gathering.

options.text: Partly through Israel drawing the nations to the temple.
options.note: A real Old Testament hope, but its fulfilment is in Christ and the gospel.

options.text: They are never gathered; they stay divided forever.
options.note: Revelation shows every nation gathered before the Lamb.

quiz.q: What does the name Peleg signal in the genealogy?

options.text: Division, because in his days the earth was divided.
options.note: The name carries a trace of the scattering narrated at Babel.

options.text: Blessing, because his line was especially favoured.
options.note: The name means division, not blessing.

options.text: Mighty one, like Nimrod.
options.note: That title belongs to Nimrod, not Peleg.

options.text: A hint that judgment and scattering are woven into the story.
options.note: This is the right implication, though the name itself specifically means division.

questions: The whole human race is one family here. How does it change the way you see people of other nations to know they are your distant relatives under one God?
God rules over Babylon and Nineveh as surely as over His own people. Where do you need to trust that God is still Lord over powers that look bigger than you?
The chapter quietly narrows to one family so a Saviour can come for all. Where are you tempted to think God only cares about the big and powerful, not the small and chosen?
The nations scattered here are gathered again in Christ. Who in your life feels far off and unreachable, and how might the gospel be for them too?
Nimrod built cities to make himself great. What are you building, and is it for your own name or for God's?

translationNotes.note: The refrain according to their languages assumes the scattering of Babel that follows in chapter eleven; Genesis 10 gives the outcome, chapter eleven gives the cause.

The phrase a mighty hunter before the LORD can be read positively as in the sight of the LORD or negatively as in defiance of the LORD, and Nimrod's link to Babylon inclines many toward the ominous sense.
